plot(s)

summary(summary(sensemakr(m, treatment = "EU:Post_2021")))
## Sensitivity Analysis to Unobserved Confounding
## 
## Model Formula: log(Applicants) ~ EU:Post_2021 + as.factor(Domicile_named_country) + 
##     as.factor(Year)
## 
## Null hypothesis: q = 1 and reduce = TRUE 
## -- This means we are considering biases that reduce the absolute value of the current estimate.
## -- The null hypothesis deemed problematic is H0:tau = 0 
## 
## Unadjusted Estimates of 'EU:Post_2021': 
##   Coef. estimate: -1.2651 
##   Standard Error: 0.047 
##   t-value (H0:tau = 0): -26.9263 
## 
## Sensitivity Statistics:
##   Partial R2 of treatment with outcome: 0.6129 
##   Robustness Value, q = 1: 0.6949 
##   Robustness Value, q = 1, alpha = 0.05: 0.67 
## 
## Verbal interpretation of sensitivity statistics:
## 
## -- Partial R2 of the treatment with the outcome: an extreme confounder (orthogonal to the covariates) that explains 100% of the residual variance of the outcome, would need to explain at least 61.29% of the residual variance of the treatment to fully account for the observed estimated effect.
## 
## -- Robustness Value, q = 1: unobserved confounders (orthogonal to the covariates) that explain more than 69.49% of the residual variance of both the treatment and the outcome are strong enough to bring the point estimate to 0 (a bias of 100% of the original estimate). Conversely, unobserved confounders that do not explain more than 69.49% of the residual variance of both the treatment and the outcome are not strong enough to bring the point estimate to 0.
## 
## -- Robustness Value, q = 1, alpha = 0.05: unobserved confounders (orthogonal to the covariates) that explain more than 67% of the residual variance of both the treatment and the outcome are strong enough to bring the estimate to a range where it is no longer 'statistically different' from 0 (a bias of 100% of the original estimate), at the significance level of alpha = 0.05. Conversely, unobserved confounders that do not explain more than 67% of the residual variance of both the treatment and the outcome are not strong enough to bring the estimate to a range where it is no longer 'statistically different' from 0, at the significance level of alpha = 0.05.
## Length  Class   Mode 
##      0   NULL   NULL
ovb_minimal_reporting(s, format = "latex")
## \begin{table}[!h]
## \centering
## \begin{tabular}{lrrrrrr}
## \multicolumn{7}{c}{Outcome: \textit{Applicants}} \\
## \hline \hline 
## Treatment: & Est. & S.E. & t-value & $R^2_{Y \sim D |{\bf X}}$ & $RV_{q = 1}$ & $RV_{q = 1, \alpha = 0.05}$  \\ 
## \hline 
## \textit{EU:Post_2021} & -1.265 & 0.047 & -26.926 & 61.3\% & 69.5\% & 67\% \\ 
## \hline 
## df = 458 & & \multicolumn{5}{r}{ }\end{tabular}
## \end{table}

ATE wealth heterogeneity

Constructing the database

rm(list = ls())            # remove all objects
gc()                       # garbage collection
##           used  (Mb) gc trigger  (Mb) max used  (Mb)
## Ncells 3627547 193.8    6669392 356.2  6669392 356.2
## Vcells 6505255  49.7   12255594  93.6 10146294  77.5
data <- read_excel(
  "data_filtered.xlsx", 
  sheet = "full_data"
)

data <- data %>%
  filter(!Domicile_named_country %in% c(
    "St Barthelemy", "St Martin (French part)", "Reunion",
    "Martinique", "Guadeloupe", "Aland Islands",
    "Cyprus (Not otherwise specified)",
    "French West Indies (Not In Use)",
    "Canary Islands", "French Guiana"
  ))

eu_real <- c(
  "Austria", "Belgium", "Bulgaria", "Croatia", "Cyprus (European Union)",
  "Czech Republic", "Denmark", "Estonia", "Finland", "France",
  "Germany", "Greece", "Hungary",  "Italy",
  "Latvia", "Lithuania", "Luxembourg", "Malta", "Netherlands",
  "Poland", "Portugal", "Romania", "Slovakia", "Slovenia",
  "Spain", "Sweden"
)

eu_synth <- c("Australia", "Saudi Arabia", "Russian Federation", "Peru", "Kuwait", "Bahrain","Singapore","Switzerland", "Hong Kong", "United States of America",  "Thailand", "Canada", "Mauritius", "Qatar", "Norway", "Israel", "Malaysia", "Jordan", "Albania", "Serbia", "Ireland", "Jersey", "Macao","United Arab Emirates", "Vietnam [Viet Nam]", "Turkey")


data <- data %>%
  filter(Domicile_named_country %in% c(eu_real, eu_synth))

data <- data %>%
  mutate(
    real_EU  = ifelse(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real, 1, 0),
    synth_EU = ifelse(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_synth, 1, 0),
    EU_group = case_when(
      Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real ~ "Real_EU",
      Domicile_named_country %in% eu_synth ~ "Synthetic_EU",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    )
  )
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(stringr)

# === 1) LOAD RAW FILES ===
gdp_raw <- read_excel("wb/gdp.xls")
pop_raw <- read_excel("wb/population_15_65.xls")
unemp_raw <- read_excel("wb/young_unempl.xls")

# === 2) Transform to long format ===
to_panel <- function(df, varname) {
  df %>%
    rename(Domicile_named_country = 1) %>%  # rename right here
    pivot_longer(
      cols = matches("^\\d{4}$"),
      names_to = "Year",
      values_to = varname
    ) %>%
    mutate(
      Year = as.numeric(Year),
      Domicile_named_country = str_trim(Domicile_named_country)
    )
}

gdp_panel   <- to_panel(gdp_raw,  "GDP")
pop_panel   <- to_panel(pop_raw,  "Pop15_65")
unemp_panel <- to_panel(unemp_raw, "YouthUnempl")

# === 3) Merge controls into one clean panel ===
controls <- gdp_panel %>%
  full_join(pop_panel,   by = c("Domicile_named_country", "Year")) %>%
  full_join(unemp_panel, by = c("Domicile_named_country", "Year"))

data_controls <- data %>%
  left_join(controls, by = c("Domicile_named_country", "Year"))
head(controls)
data_controls <- data %>%
  left_join(controls, by = c("Domicile_named_country", "Year"))
head(data_controls)

ATE wealth heterogeneity FO

library(plm)
library(lmtest)
## Loading required package: zoo
## 
## Attaching package: 'zoo'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     as.Date, as.Date.numeric
library(sandwich)
library(stargazer)
## 
## Please cite as:
##  Hlavac, Marek (2022). stargazer: Well-Formatted Regression and Summary Statistics Tables.
##  R package version 5.2.3. https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=stargazer
library(dplyr)

# ==== Base Panel Dataset ====
data_panel <- data_controls %>%
  mutate(
    log_app = log(Applicants),
    EU = as.numeric(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real),
    Post = as.numeric(Year >= 2021),
    did = EU * Post
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(log_app))

pdata <- pdata.frame(data_panel, index = c("Domicile_named_country", "Year"))

# ==== Model Set ====

# M1 — Clean baseline DiD
m1 <- plm(log_app ~ did,
          data = pdata, model = "within", effect = "twoways")

# M2 — Add full structural controls
m2 <- plm(log_app ~ did + log(GDP) ,
          data = pdata, model = "within", effect = "twoways")

# M3 — GDP interaction
m3 <- plm(log_app ~ did*log(GDP) + Pop15_65 + YouthUnempl,
          data = pdata, model = "within", effect = "twoways")

# M4 — Youth unemployment interaction
m4 <- plm(log_app ~ did*YouthUnempl + log(GDP) + Pop15_65,
          data = pdata, model = "within", effect = "twoways")

# M5 — Demographic (youth cohort) interaction
m5 <- plm(log_app ~ did*Pop15_65 + log(GDP) + YouthUnempl,
          data = pdata, model = "within", effect = "twoways")

# M6 — Fully interacted — ALL structural channels
m6 <- plm(log_app ~ did*(log(GDP) + Pop15_65 + YouthUnempl),
          data = pdata, model = "within", effect = "twoways")

# ==== Cluster-robust SEs ====
vc <- function(model) vcovHC(model, type = "HC1", cluster = "group")

cl_m1 <- coeftest(m1, vc(m1))
cl_m2 <- coeftest(m2, vc(m2))
cl_m3 <- coeftest(m3, vc(m3))
cl_m4 <- coeftest(m4, vc(m4))
cl_m5 <- coeftest(m5, vc(m5))
cl_m6 <- coeftest(m6, vc(m6))

# ==== Publication-ready Stargazer ====

stargazer(m1, m2, m3, m4, m5, m6,
          se = list(cl_m1[, "Std. Error"],
                    cl_m2[, "Std. Error"],
                    cl_m3[, "Std. Error"],
                    cl_m4[, "Std. Error"],
                    cl_m5[, "Std. Error"],
                    cl_m6[, "Std. Error"]), type="text")
## 
## =====================================================================================================================================================================
##                                                                                  Dependent variable:                                                                 
##                 -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
##                                                                                        log_app                                                                       
##                           (1)                      (2)                      (3)                      (4)                      (5)                      (6)           
## ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## did                    -1.265***                -1.213***                -9.313***                -1.103***                -6.992***                -11.485***       
##                         (0.114)                  (0.109)                  (2.319)                  (0.255)                  (2.687)                  (3.821)         
##                                                                                                                                                                      
## log(GDP)                                          -0.396                  -1.253**                -1.765***                -1.856***                -1.299***        
##                                                  (0.528)                  (0.587)                  (0.656)                  (0.614)                  (0.491)         
##                                                                                                                                                                      
## Pop15_65                                                                  0.092**                  0.096**                  0.084**                  0.082**         
##                                                                           (0.040)                  (0.044)                  (0.040)                  (0.040)         
##                                                                                                                                                                      
## did:YouthUnempl                                                                                     -0.001                                            0.008          
##                                                                                                    (0.011)                                           (0.010)         
##                                                                                                                                                                      
## YouthUnempl                                                              -0.023***                -0.022***                -0.023***                -0.022***        
##                                                                           (0.006)                  (0.006)                  (0.006)                  (0.005)         
##                                                                                                                                                                      
## did:log(GDP)                                                              0.754***                                                                   0.680**         
##                                                                           (0.213)                                                                    (0.295)         
##                                                                                                                                                                      
## did:Pop15_65                                                                                                                0.091**                   0.044          
##                                                                                                                             (0.042)                  (0.058)         
##                                                                                                                                                                      
## ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## Observations              520                      450                      387                      387                      387                      387           
## R2                       0.613                    0.610                    0.702                    0.662                    0.684                    0.708          
## Adjusted R2              0.561                    0.551                    0.653                    0.606                    0.631                    0.658          
## F Statistic     725.026*** (df = 1; 458) 305.015*** (df = 2; 390) 156.118*** (df = 5; 331) 129.582*** (df = 5; 331) 143.060*** (df = 5; 331) 114.214*** (df = 7; 329)
## =====================================================================================================================================================================
## Note:                                                                                                                                     *p<0.1; **p<0.05; ***p<0.01

Distribution graph

library(dplyr)
library(lattice)

eu_countries <- pdata %>% filter(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_real)
eu_control   <- pdata %>% filter(Domicile_named_country %in% eu_synth)

eu_countries$group <- "EU"
eu_control$group   <- "Control"

df_plot <- rbind(eu_countries, eu_control) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(as.character(Year))) 

densityplot(~ GDP, group = group, data = df_plot,
            plot.points = FALSE, auto.key = list(columns = 2), lwd = 2)

densityplot(~ Applicants, group = group, data = df_plot,
            plot.points = FALSE, auto.key = list(columns = 2), lwd = 2)

densityplot(~ Accepted_applicants, group = group, data = df_plot,
            plot.points = FALSE, auto.key = list(columns = 2), lwd = 2)

densityplot(~ GDP + Applicants + Accepted_applicants,
            group = group, data = df_plot,
            plot.points = FALSE, auto.key = list(columns = 2), lwd = 2,
            layout = c(1, 3), as.table = TRUE,
            scales = list(relation = "free"),
            xlab = "")

Descriptives (excel)

library(dplyr)
library(openxlsx)

library(dplyr)
library(openxlsx)

desc_country <- df_plot %>%
  group_by(group, Domicile_named_country) %>%
  summarise(
    N = n(),
    
    GDP_mean = mean(GDP, na.rm = TRUE),
    GDP_sd   = sd(GDP, na.rm = TRUE),
    
    Applicants_mean = mean(Applicants, na.rm = TRUE),
    Applicants_sd   = sd(Applicants, na.rm = TRUE),
    
    Accepted_mean = mean(Accepted_applicants, na.rm = TRUE),
    Accepted_sd   = sd(Accepted_applicants, na.rm = TRUE),
    
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(group, Domicile_named_country)
desc_country_wide <- desc_country %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(
    names_from = group,
    values_from = c(
      GDP_mean, GDP_sd,
      Applicants_mean, Applicants_sd,
      Accepted_mean, Accepted_sd
    )
  )
write.xlsx(
  list(
    "Country-level (long)" = desc_country,
    "Country-level (wide)" = desc_country_wide
  ),
  file = "Country_Descriptives_Control_vs_Synthetic.xlsx",
  rowNames = FALSE
)
h <- hist(
  eu_control$GDP,
  breaks = "FD",
  freq = FALSE,
  right = FALSE,
  plot = TRUE 
)

plot(
  h$mids, h$density,
  type = "l",
  lwd = 2,
  xlab = "GDP per cap",
  ylab = "Density"
)

Note on how this was constructed: Assuming that there are K bin boundaries \(b_0\leq b_1 \leq \dots\leq b_K\) separated into specific intervals \(I_p=[b_p,b_{p+1}]\), the midpoint of those intervals is \(m_p=\frac{b_p+b_{p+1}}{2}\) and the bins are defined as \(\Delta_p=b_{p+1}-b_p\). \(freq_p\) are the number of observations filling into bin \(p\). The densities are given by:

\[ density_p=\frac{freq_p}{obs \cdot \Delta_p } \] where \(obs\) denotes the number of observations in the sample. By construction the histogram integrates to unity \(\left( E[x]=\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(x)x dx=1 \right)\), hence the discrete version of this is given by:

\[ \sum_{p=1}^K density_p\cdot \Delta_p \] where \(bin_p\) is \(b_{j+1}-b_j\) (i.e. the sum of the probabilities of observing bin p somewhere in the distribution). To show the unity summation consider:

\[ \sum_{1}^K density_p\cdot \Delta_p=\sum_{1}^K \frac{freq_p}{obs \cdot \Delta_p }\cdot \Delta_p= \frac{\sum_{1}^K freq_p}{n}=n/n=1 \]

# Total observations
n <- sum(h$counts)

# Bin widths
binwidth <- diff(h$breaks)

# Manual density calculation
density_manual <- h$counts / (n * binwidth)

# Verification table
density_check <- data.frame(
  bin_left   = h$breaks[-length(h$breaks)],
  bin_right  = h$breaks[-1],
  midpoint   = h$mids,
  count      = h$counts,
  binwidth   = binwidth,
  density_R  = h$density,
  density_manual = density_manual,
  diff = h$density - density_manual
)

density_check

R says 13 rows hence 13 bins. Lets test for some of them. The second bin starts from 10000 to 20000 and has a midpoint of 15000, consisting o of 18 obs.

delta_2=20000-10000
density_2=18/(sum(h$counts)*delta_2)

density_2
## [1] 8.333333e-06

we are fine then.
